svg.title: Use Case background — Cost: contact-centre support agent at a large insurer
svg.desc: Background for the Cost use case. The client is a large UK life and pensions insurer (illustrative, not based on any specific company): ~12M customers, ~£280bn assets under administration, several consumer brands, ~$0.7M/year estimated LLM spend. The AI is a contact-centre support agent handling ~12,000 conversations a day — drafting, summarising, classifying and retrieving policy info for human advisors. Every call carries a large ~8k-token static prefix against a tiny question, so input tokens dominate the bill — the cost lever this use case targets.
svg.text: Use Case — Cost: the support agent
Background · who the client is, and what the AI is trying to do · Standard GC3B3-01
THE CLIENT
illustrative — a large UK life & pensions insurer (not a specific company)
~12M customers
life, pensions & savings
~£280bn assets under administration
Several consumer brands
document- & contact-heavy → ideal GenAI footprint
~$0.7M / year estimated LLM spend
range ~$150k (pilots) to several £M (broad rollout)
The reason cost discipline matters:
a contact-and-document business runs millions of
model calls — small per-call waste becomes a real bill.
WHAT THE AI DOES
contact-centre support agent — "agent assist"
~12,000 conversations / day
helping human advisors, not replacing them
On each turn, for the advisor it:
drafts a reply to the customer
summarises the case so far
classifies the query / routes it
retrieves the right policy & product info
Goal:
cut handling time and keep answers consistent
and accurate across thousands of advisors.
Each call carries ~8k tokens of fixed context.
THE COST SHAPE — why this is a Cost case
› Every call re-sends a big static prefix — system prompt + tool defs + few-shot examples + retrieved policy (~8k tokens) — against a tiny question.
› Input tokens dominate the bill (~75–80% of cost). That unchanging prefix is the single biggest cost lever in the system.
› Caching the static prefix so it bills at a fraction is exactly what this use case (GC3B3-01) targets — plausibly ~$250k–$450k/year.
figures illustrative — not based on any specific company
